Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd try to set aside time on weekends since I work during the week, but it never really stuck consistently. I'd sit down with my materials, maybe do some reading or work through practice problems with my hands, but I wouldn't say I had a real organized system. Most weeks I just did the minimum to keep moving forward.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I'd go weeks without doing much, then cram when something was coming up. Between work and my other commitments, I couldn't really stick to a regular schedule. Deadlines were basically my only real motivator to actually sit down and study.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got an email reminder. Sometimes I'd check in just to see if there was anything new posted, but I wasn't one of those people who logged in every day. It was more like a weekly check-in at best, sometimes less.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I never got to the point where logging into the LMS felt automatic or natural. It always felt like something I had to remind myself to do rather than something that was just part of my day. The mindfulness exercises they had us do helped me feel calmer about the course, but they didn't change my LMS habits much.
Q5: The video lectures were somewhat useful because I could watch them at my own pace and pause to take notes. I also liked when there were practical examples I could work through with my hands, like practice problems or simulations. Those felt more engaging than just reading walls of text.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. They felt like busy work to me, and I didn't see how posting there would help me learn the actual material. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I was already struggling to keep up with the core content. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment, I probably didn't touch it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to something I'd be graded on. If I couldn't figure out why it mattered or how it would help me pass, I'd get bored and move on. I needed things to feel practical and hands-on, not just theoretical busy work.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, I probably wasn't going to do it unless I was really interested. Required activities at least had the pressure of affecting my grade, so I'd make myself do them even if I wasn't feeling motivated.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and lecture slides and study from those on my phone during breaks at work since I don't have reliable internet at home. I also used physical notes a lot because writing things out by hand helps me remember better. A lot of my actual studying happened completely offline.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing printed notes or working through problems on paper that wouldn't show up anywhere online. I'd also sometimes study with a coworker who was taking similar classes, and we'd quiz each other verbally. None of that would ever appear in any system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then work with it offline for days or even weeks. My internet situation meant I had to plan ahead and grab everything I needed when I had access. So one login might represent hours of studying that happened later.
Q12: Around midterms it got really hard. I was picking up extra shifts at work and dealing with some personal stuff, and the course material was getting more complex. I just felt bored and disconnected from it all. The mindfulness intervention helped my mood, but it didn't magically give me more hours in the day.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired from work, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and watch videos. Fatigue was probably my biggest obstacle. When I had competing responsibilities, coursework always came last because it felt less urgent than paying bills or handling real-life stuff.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open a video or reading and just zone out completely. I'd be staring at the screen but not absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere. Those sessions felt like a waste of time, but at least I could tell myself I tried.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was low motivation, part of it was feeling overwhelmed by how much content there was. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing again.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd log in way more frequently in the days before something was due. Without a deadline looming, I had no real urgency to engage with the material. It wasn't a healthy pattern, but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got feedback on one assignment, I was already behind on the next thing. I appreciated when instructors gave comments, but I rarely went back to review them in depth. It just felt like too much effort.
Q18: The course structure was pretty overwhelming with 18 different online courses to manage. I couldn't keep track of everything, so I just focused on whatever was most immediately due. A simpler structure with clearer priorities would have helped me stay more engaged throughout.
Q19: The sheer amount of content made engagement harder. I'm a hands-on learner, and a lot of the course was just reading and watching, which doesn't stick for me. When there were interactive elements or practical exercises, I engaged more. But those were rare.
Q20: I think some students might be like me, where they're logging in and clicking around trying to figure out what they need to do, but not actually completing things. Or they're watching videos passively without really learning. High activity doesn't mean high quality engagement.
Q21: That makes sense to me because a lot of real studying happens offline. Someone could download everything, study from printed materials, and only log in to submit work. Their activity would look low, but they might actually be learning effectively in their own way.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how much time you spent with downloaded materials. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the conversations with other people about the content.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, it would mean feeling curious and motivated rather than bored and obligated. I wasn't really engaged this semester, I was just complying with requirements.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with work stress and limited internet access, or that I learn best by doing things with my hands rather than reading screens. It also wouldn't show that the mindfulness intervention actually improved my mood significantly, even if it didn't change my grades. My circumstances mattered a lot.
